params.description: Key on right graph highlights

steps.text: Here's a hint: The scale tells us the VALUE of each symbol, not how many symbols there are. Look at what the key says.

params.description: Key on right graph highlights and animates to show 1 symbol = 5 items

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. The key says 'Each fruit symbol = 5 votes.' That means every single symbol stands for 5 items. So the scale is 5.

steps.text: Let's see how 20 items become symbols when the scale is 5. Watch — we'll group by 5s.

Items group into 4 groups of 5 each. Guide counts aloud during animation.

steps.text: 5, 10, 15, 20. That's 4 groups of 5.

params.description: 4 groups transform into 4 star symbols stacking vertically in Swings column.

steps.text: 20 items equals 4 symbols. Each symbol shows 5.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values.
Vertical picture graph with Swings column showing 4 symbols. Other columns empty. Key visible: Each star = 5.

incorrects.description: Student selected 4 — counted the symbols instead of their total value

steps.text: You counted the symbols. Each symbol represents 5 items, not 1. Try counting by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20.

incorrects.description: Student selected 9 — possibly added 4 symbols + scale of 5

steps.text: Let's think about this together. We don't add the scale to the number of symbols. Each symbol stands for 5, so count by 5s four times.

incorrects.description: Student selected 15 — stopped one symbol short when skip counting

steps.text: You're getting there. Count all 4 symbols. Start at 5 for the first symbol, then keep going: 5, 10, 15, and one more.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Each symbol in Swings column highlights sequentially as skip counting progresses: first symbol shows 5, second shows 10, third shows 15, fourth shows 20

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Each symbol represents 5. Count by 5s: first symbol is 5, second is 10, third is 15, fourth is 20. The answer is 20.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong number of symbols on first attempt

steps.text: Let's look closely—count by fives to 25.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong number of symbols on first attempt

steps.text: Check the data table. How many did Sandbox get?

params.description: System places 2 symbols in Sandbox column

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Sandbox has 10. Each symbol stands for 5. I'll count by 5s: 5, 10. That's 2 symbols for Sandbox. There we go.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

steps.text: Let's look closely—which column is tallest?

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count the symbols in each column. Monkey Bars has 5 symbols—more than any other activity.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

You made a picture graph with scale of 5. Now let's see ANOTHER way to show the exact same data.

elements.description: Completed vertical picture graph from Section 1. Favorite Playground Activities data.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'In the symbols' — confusing picture graph symbols with bar graph axis

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Bar graphs don't use symbols. Look at the numbers written along the side — that's where the 5s are.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'In the title' — looking in wrong location for scale information

steps.text: Here's a hint: The title tells us what the graph is about, not how to count. Look at the numbers along the side of the graph.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Axis numbers pulse in sequence: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. The axis is the side of the graph where the numbers are written: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. That's where you see the 5s — counting by 5s right up the side.

incorrects.description: Student selected 3 — confused bar height with number of symbols on picture graph

params.description: Guideline draws from top of Slides bar to axis at 15

steps.text: You counted the picture graph symbols. In a bar graph, read where the bar ENDS on the axis. Where does the Slides bar end?

incorrects.description: Student selected 10 — read axis at wrong position

params.description: Guideline draws from top of Slides bar to axis at 15

steps.text: Let's look closely at where the bar ends. Follow the top of the Slides bar across to the axis. What number does it reach?

incorrects.description: Student selected 20 — read maximum axis value instead of bar height

params.description: Guideline draws from top of Slides bar to axis at 15

steps.text: That's the highest number on the axis. We need where the Slides bar ENDS. Look at the top of the bar—what number is it touching?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Guideline draws from top of Slides bar to axis at 15, then highlights the 15 label

steps.text: Let me show you how to read bar height. I look at the top of the Slides bar. I follow it across to the axis. It ends at 15. The HEIGHT is 15. Click 15.

incorrects.description: Student selected 21 — counting by 1s instead of by the axis scale

steps.text: Let's think about this together. This axis counts by 5s, not by 1s. Look at the numbers: 5, 10, 15, 20... what's the next jump of 5?

incorrects.description: Student selected 30 — skipped ahead two intervals instead of one

steps.text: Here's a hint: 30 comes after 25. We're looking for the number right after 20 — just one jump up.

incorrects.description: Student selected 22 — counting by 2s instead of by the axis scale

steps.text: You're working on it. This axis doesn't count by 2s — it counts by 5s. Each mark is 5 more than the one before.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Axis marks light up in sequence: 5, 10, 15, 20, then 25 pulses

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. The next number after 20 is 25 — one more jump of 5. The answer is 25.

steps.text: Check the table. What number is next to Pizza?

incorrects.description: Student set bar at wrong height three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: Pizza bar rises to height 20, aligned with axis mark

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. The table says Pizza has 20 votes. I find 20 on the axis—count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20. Then I set the bar to end right there at 20. See how that works?

incorrects.description: Student set at least one bar incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Check the table. What number is next to Salad? What number is next to Pasta?

params.description: System sets Salad bar to 15 and Pasta bar to 25

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Salad has 15 votes, so I set its bar to 15. Pasta has 25 votes, so I set its bar to 25. Each bar matches the number from the table.

incorrects.description: Student selected 4 — counted symbols instead of using scale

steps.text: You're thinking about this a bit more. The key tells us each symbol equals 5 students. So 5 symbols means 5 groups of 5.

incorrects.description: Student selected 20 — miscounted by fives

steps.text: Here's a hint: count by 5s carefully. Each symbol represents 5 students. Start at 5 and count up to 25.

incorrects.description: Student selected 25 — confused the value with the number of symbols

steps.text: Let's think about this together. 25 is the total number of students. But each symbol stands for 5 students, so we need fewer symbols.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: System highlights Pasta symbols one by one while counting by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25. Shows 5 total symbols.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Each symbol equals 5 students. Watch as I count: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25. That's 5 symbols to show 25 students. The answer is 5.

incorrects.description: Student selected 10 — misread scale or counted intervals incorrectly

params.description: Guidelines draw from top of Tacos and Salad bars to axis
Guidelines draw from top of Tacos and Salad bars to axis

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Tacos reaches 30 and Salad reaches 15. The difference between those numbers is how many MORE.

incorrects.description: Student selected 30 — read Tacos bar height instead of finding the difference

params.description: Guideline draws from top of Tacos bar to axis

steps.text: Here's a hint: 30 is how many chose Tacos total. But the question asks how many MORE than Salad—that means find the difference.

incorrects.description: Student selected 45 — added Tacos and Salad instead of finding the difference

steps.text: You're working on it. The word MORE means we're comparing, not adding. How many more means subtract: Tacos minus Salad.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Guidelines draw from both bars showing 30 for Tacos, 15 for Salad, difference highlighted
Guidelines draw from both bars showing 30 for Tacos, 15 for Salad, difference highlighted

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Tacos has 30 students. Salad has 15 students. To find how many MORE, we subtract: 30 minus 15 equals 15. The answer is 15.
So far, some parts of the graphs were already filled in for you. Now let's see if you can build a whole graph on your own.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong number of symbols on first attempt

steps.text: Let's look at this problem again. Check how many books Liam read.

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong number of symbols on second attempt

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Liam read 35 books. Each symbol stands for 5 books. Count by 5s: how many 5s in 35?

incorrects.description: Student placed wrong number of symbols three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: System places symbols one at a time in Liam row while counting: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35. 7 symbols total.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Liam read 35 books. Each symbol is 5 books. I count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35. That's 7 symbols for 35 books. Now click to place 7 symbols.

incorrects.description: Student set at least one row incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Let's look closely—check the data table for each person's number.

incorrects.description: Student set at least one row incorrectly on second attempt

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Emma read 20 books. Each symbol shows 5, so count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20—that's 4 symbols.

incorrects.description: Student set at least one row incorrectly three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: System places correct number of symbols: Emma 4, Noah 5, Olivia 8

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Emma: 20 books, skip-count by 5s—that's 4 symbols. Noah: 25 books—that's 5 symbols. Olivia: 40 books—that's 8 symbols. Each symbol represents 5 books.

incorrects.description: Student selected 40 — reported Olivia's total instead of the difference

steps.text: You found Olivia's total. The question asks how many FEWER Emma read—that means the difference between the two amounts.

incorrects.description: Student selected 60 — added the two amounts instead of finding the difference

steps.text: Let's think about this together. FEWER means we subtract, not add. Count Emma's books, then Olivia's books, then find the difference.

incorrects.description: Student selected 15 — miscounted or used incorrect values

steps.text: Here's a hint: Emma has 20 books. Olivia has 40 books. To find how many FEWER, subtract Emma's amount from Olivia's amount.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: System counts Emma's row showing 20 books, then counts Olivia's row showing 40 books, then demonstrates subtraction: 40 - 20 = 20

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. First, I count Emma's books—that's 20. Then I count Olivia's books—that's 40. To find how many FEWER Emma read, I subtract: 40 minus 20 equals 20. Emma read 20 fewer books than Olivia.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1 — uses the most symbols, not the fewest

steps.text: Let's think about this together. With scale of 1, Olivia had 40 symbols. That's a lot. Which scale had fewer?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2 — uses fewer than scale of 1 but more than scale of 5

steps.text: You're working on it. Scale of 2 used 20 symbols for Olivia. But scale of 5 used even fewer — just 8.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Graph highlights key: Each star = 5. Olivia's row pulses showing 8 symbols.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Scale of 1 used 40 symbols for Olivia. Scale of 2 used 20. Scale of 5 used just 8 — the fewest. The bigger the scale number, the fewer symbols you need. Click Scale of 5.

incorrects.description: Student selected 36 — counting by ones instead of recognizing the skip-counting pattern

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Look at all the numbers on the axis: 0, 5, 10, 15. They're not going up by 1 — what pattern do you see?

incorrects.description: Student selected 45 — skipped ahead instead of finding the next number in sequence

steps.text: Here's a hint: 45 is on the axis, but it comes later. What number sits between 35 and 45?

incorrects.description: Student selected 30 — went backwards on the axis instead of forward

steps.text: You're getting there. 30 comes before 35 on the axis. The question asks what comes after — which number is next?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Axis labels light up in sequence: 30, 35, 40

steps.text: This one takes a bit of thought, so let's work through it together. The axis counts by 5s. After 30 comes 35. After 35 comes 40. See how that works?

incorrects.description: Student set one or more bars incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Let's look closely—check each bar against the data table.

incorrects.description: Student set one or more bars incorrectly on second attempt

steps.text: Here's a hint: Match each bar to its value in the table. Saturday should reach 35, Sunday 30, Monday 40, Tuesday 25.

incorrects.description: Student set one or more bars incorrectly three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: System sets all bars to correct lengths: Saturday 35, Sunday 30, Monday 40, Tuesday 25

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. I read each value from the table—Saturday 35, Sunday 30, Monday 40, Tuesday 25—then set each bar to match. Now you see the complete graph.

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 — confused by the scale; counted jumps instead of reading values

steps.text: You're working on it. Each jump is 5 minutes. Saturday's bar goes to 35, not 5. Count by 5s to find each day's total.

incorrects.description: Student selected 35 — read Saturday's value only, didn't add Sunday

steps.text: Here's a hint: 35 is Saturday's minutes. The question asks for Saturday AND Sunday IN ALL—you need to add both days together.

incorrects.description: Student selected 70 — added Saturday and Sunday but made a calculation error (35+30=65, not 70)

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Saturday is 35 minutes, Sunday is 30. When we add them: 35 plus 30 equals 65, not 70.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Saturday bar (35) and Sunday bar (30) pulse in sequence, then combined total of 65 appears

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Saturday's bar reaches 35 minutes. Sunday's bar reaches 30 minutes. To find the total IN ALL, we add them: 35 plus 30 equals 65 minutes. The answer is 65.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'They both use symbols' — incorrect because bar graphs use bars, not symbols

steps.text: Here's a hint: picture graphs use symbols, but bar graphs use bars instead. Look at what both graphs show on the side.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'They look the same' — incorrect because the graphs have different visual formats

steps.text: Let's think about this together. These graphs look different—one uses symbols, one uses bars. But check the numbers on the side of each graph.

incorrects.description: Student selected 'They both have 5 categories' — incorrect because each graph has 4 categories

steps.text: You're working on it. Count the categories—both graphs have 4, not 5. The number 5 appears somewhere else on both graphs.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Scale marker pulses on picture graph showing each symbol = 5
Scale numbers pulse on bar graph axis showing 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Look at the picture graph—each symbol equals 5. Now look at the bar graph—the numbers go up by 5. Both graphs use the same scale of 5, just in different ways.
You made picture graphs and bar graphs, both with scale of 5. You used skip-counting to figure out symbols and bar heights. Let's see what you know.
